text: Il Servizio di Chirurgia Generale dell'Ospedale Universitario Marquès de Val d'Isilla presents robotic sigmadectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis using the Wige and Pop technique.
Il Servizio di Chirurgia Generale dell'Ospedale Universitario Marquès de Val d'Isilla presents robotic sigmadectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis using the Wige and Pop technique.

The majority of left-sided robotic colorectal procedures are associated to a laparotomy before the anastomosis fashioning. This lengthens the procedure, obliging the surgeon to leave his robotic console.

We recently adopted a new anastomotic method with an intracorporeal insertion of the anvil at the beginning of the operation, thus postponing the abdominal incision.

The patient is located in a supine position in a twenty-five rehearsal in Dellenburg, legs apart.
The patient is positioned supine with legs apart in a 25-degree Trendelenburg position. The robotic surgical system is docked over the patient's left hip, ready for the procedure.
The robotic surgical system is docked over the patient's left hip, positioning the robotic arms for the colectomy procedure.
Three robotic trocars are positioned: one in the apophysis, another in the epigastrium, and the third in the left flank, facilitating access for the robotic arms during the colectomy procedure.
Three robotic trocars are positioned: one in the umbilical region, another in the epigastrium, and the third in the left flank, facilitating access for the robotic arms during the procedure.
After dividing the mesentery, the surgeon uses a fur fly to assess the perfusion of the colon, visually inspecting the tissue for adequate blood flow.
After dividing the mesentery, the surgeon uses a fluorescent dye to assess perfusion. A colotomy is performed using a monopolar curved scissor, cutting through the colon tissue.
The surgeon performs a colotomy using a monopolar scissor, making an incision in the colon tissue.
The surgeon inserts a handwheel with a V-lock suture attached into the lumen of the colon, preparing for further manipulation or closure.
The surgeon inserts the handwheel with a V-lock attached to its spike into the lumen of the colon, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the colotum using a running V-lock trio suture, securing the tissue with precise stitching.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the colon just proximal to the colotomy site, ensuring precise division of the tissue.
The robotic stapler is used to transect the colon just proximal to the colotomy site, completing the separation of the colon segment.
The surgeon ensures that the anvil anchoring the V-lock suture remains intact and uncut during the procedure.
The anvil anchoring the V-lock remains intact as the surgeon completes the transaction of the colon using robotic instruments.
The surgeon completes the transection of the colon using the robotic stapler, finalizing the separation of the colon at the designated site.
The surgeon uses a hot scissor to cut the staple line between the spike and the suture within the colon lumen.
The surgeon uses a hot scissor to cut the staple line between the spike and the suture. The spike is then gently removed from the colon and detached from the hand-wheel.
The surgeon gently removes the spike from the colon, following the completion of the transaction and closure of the colotomy site.
The colon is finally detached from the hand-wheel, completing the separation process initiated by previous steps.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn anastomosis, joining the two ends of the bowel together with sutures.
The surgeon performs a hand-to-hand anastomosis of the colon, using the forefly device to assess the perfusion of the anastomotic site.
The surgeon uses the forefly device to assess the perfusion of the anastomosis, visually inspecting the blood flow to ensure adequate circulation in the newly connected bowel segments.
The surgeon performs a higher leak test on the anastomosis, visually assessing for any signs of leakage at the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the colorectal anastomosis, using a flexible endoscope to visually assess the integrity and perfusion of the anastomotic site.
The surgeon uses a flexible endoscope to visually assess the colorectal anastomosis, inspecting the integrity and perfusion of the surgical connection.
Twenty patients underwent this robotic surgical technique.

Twenty patients underwent this robotic surgical technique. Only in two cases we observed a disposition of the anvil inside the lumen of the colon. No anastomotic complication and no wound infection occurred in the post-op course.
